item.name: Add
GitHub Add Assignees to an Issue

request.name: GitHub Add Assignees to an Issue

description.content: The Add Assignees To An Issue operation is a POST request to the /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/assignees endpoint that allows you to add one or more assignees to an existing GitHub issue. You must specify the repository owner, repository name, and issue number in the URL path, and provide an array of usernames in the request body to designate which GitHub users should be assigned to the issue. This operation requires appropriate permissions to modify the issue, and the specified users must have access to the repository. Upon successful execution, the API returns the updated issue object including the newly added assignees in the assignees field, making it useful for task delegation and collaboration workflows within GitHub repositories.

variable.description: (Required) The account owner of the repository. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The name of the repository without the `.git` extension. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The number that identifies the issue.
Self-hosted Enterprise Server hostname
Self-hosted Enterprise Server protocol

response.name: Response

variable.description: (Required) The account owner of the repository. The name is not case sensitive.

Self-hosted Enterprise Server protocol

item.name: GitHub Add Labels to an Issue

request.name: GitHub Add Labels to an Issue

description.content: This POST endpoint adds one or more labels to a specific issue in a GitHub repository by specifying the repository owner, repository name, and issue number in the URL path. The request body should contain an array of label names to be added to the issue. Labels help categorize and organize issues for better project management and filtering. The authenticated user must have push access to the repository to add labels. Upon successful execution, the API returns a 200 status code with an array of all labels currently assigned to the issue, including both the newly added labels and any existing ones. This operation is idempotent, meaning adding a label that already exists on the issue will not create a duplicate.

variable.description: (Required) The account owner of the repository. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The name of the repository without the `.git` extension. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The number that identifies the issue.
Self-hosted Enterprise Server hostname
Self-hosted Enterprise Server protocol

(Required) The number that identifies the issue.

Self-hosted Enterprise Server protocol

item.name: All
GitHub Remove All Labels from an Issue

request.name: GitHub Remove All Labels from an Issue

description.content: This API operation removes all labels that have been applied to a specific issue in a GitHub repository. By sending a DELETE request to the endpoint with the repository owner, repository name, and issue number, all existing labels associated with that issue will be cleared in a single operation. This is useful when you need to reset an issue's categorization or when reorganizing your labeling system, allowing you to start fresh without having to remove labels individually. The operation requires appropriate authentication and write access to the repository's issues.

variable.description: (Required) The account owner of the repository. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The name of the repository without the `.git` extension. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The number that identifies the issue.

GitHub Check if User Can Be Assigned to Issue

request.name: GitHub Check if User Can Be Assigned to Issue

description.content:
Checks if a user has permission to be assigned to a specific issue.

If the `assignee` can be assigned to this issue, a `204` status code with no content is returned.

Otherwise a `404` status code is returned.

item.name: GitHub Remove Assignees from an Issue

request.name: GitHub Remove Assignees from an Issue

description.content: This API operation removes one or more assignees from a specified GitHub issue within a repository. By making a DELETE request to the endpoint with the repository owner, repository name, and issue number, you can provide a list of usernames in the request body to unassign those users from the issue. The authenticated user must have push access to the repository to perform this action, and only users who are currently assigned to the issue can be removed. Upon successful execution, the API returns the updated issue object with the modified assignees list, allowing you to confirm which users remain assigned to the issue.

variable.description: (Required) The account owner of the repository. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The name of the repository without the `.git` extension. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The number that identifies the issue.
Self-hosted Enterprise Server hostname

item.name: Checks
GitHub Check if User Can Be Assigned to Issue

request.name: GitHub Check if User Can Be Assigned to Issue

description.content:
Checks if a user has permission to be assigned to a specific issue.

If the `assignee` can be assigned to this issue, a `204` status code with no content is returned.

Otherwise a `404` status code is returned.

request.name: GitHub Update an Issue Comment

description.content:
You can use the REST API to update comments on issues and pull requests. Every pull request is an issue, but not every issue is a pull request.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

item.name: Comments
GitHub List Issue Comments for Repository

request.name: GitHub List Issue Comments for Repository

description.content:
You can use the REST API to list comments on issues and pull requests for a repository. Every pull request is an issue, but not every issue is a pull request.

By default, issue comments are ordered by ascending ID.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

query.description: The property to sort the results by.
Either `asc` or `desc`. Ignored without the `sort` parameter.
Only show results that were last updated after the given time. This is a timestamp in [ISO 8601](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601) format: `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ`.

The number of results per page (max 100). For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

The page number of the results to fetch. For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

item.name: GitHub List Issue Comments

request.name: GitHub List Issue Comments

description.content:
You can use the REST API to list comments on issues and pull requests. Every pull request is an issue, but not every issue is a pull request.

Issue comments are ordered by ascending ID.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.

item.name: GitHub Delete an Issue Reaction

request.name: GitHub Delete an Issue Reaction

description.content: The DELETE operation on the /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/reactions/{reaction_id} endpoint allows authenticated users to remove a specific reaction from a GitHub issue. This endpoint requires the repository owner name, repository name, issue number, and the unique reaction ID to identify and delete the particular reaction. Users can only delete reactions they have created, and the operation requires appropriate authentication and permissions to access the repository and modify reactions. Upon successful deletion, the API returns a 204 No Content status code, confirming the reaction has been removed from the specified issue.

item.name: Events
GitHub List Issue Events for Repository

request.name: GitHub List Issue Events for Repository

description.content: The List Issue Events For Repository endpoint retrieves a paginated list of all issue events that have occurred within a specific repository. This GET operation accepts the repository owner and repository name as path parameters and returns event data such as when issues were opened, closed, reopened, labeled, assigned, or had other state changes applied to them. The endpoint supports pagination through query parameters and requires appropriate authentication to access private repositories, making it useful for tracking issue activity history, generating analytics, or monitoring repository engagement patterns across all issues rather than individual ones.

item.name: GitHub Get an Issue Event

request.name: GitHub Get an Issue Event

description.content: The GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/events/{event_id} endpoint retrieves detailed information about a specific event associated with an issue in a GitHub repository. This operation requires the repository owner's username, the repository name, and the unique event identifier to fetch data about actions performed on issues such as labels being added or removed, assignments, milestone changes, comments, and state transitions. The endpoint returns a comprehensive event object containing metadata like the event type, actor who triggered the event, creation timestamp, and relevant context specific to that event type, making it useful for tracking the complete history and audit trail of changes made to issues within a repository.

variable.description: (Required) The account owner of the repository. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The name of the repository without the `.git` extension. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required)

item.name: GitHub List Issue Events

request.name: GitHub List Issue Events

description.content: The List Issue Events operation retrieves a chronological list of all events that have occurred on a specific issue within a GitHub repository. By making a GET request to /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/events, developers can access a comprehensive timeline of activities such as labels being added or removed, issue assignments, milestone changes, references from other issues or pull requests, and state changes like when the issue was closed or reopened. This endpoint is particularly useful for tracking the complete history and lifecycle of an issue, understanding how it evolved over time, and gathering data for analytics or auditing purposes. The response includes detailed information about each event type, the actor who triggered it, and the timestamp when it occurred, making it valuable for building issue management tools, generating reports, or implementing custom notification systems.

query.description: The number of results per page (max 100). For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

The page number of the results to fetch. For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

item.name: GitHub List Timeline Events for an Issue

request.name: GitHub List Timeline Events for an Issue

description.content: The GitHub Issues API endpoint `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/timeline` allows you to retrieve a chronological list of all timeline events associated with a specific issue in a repository. This GET operation returns a comprehensive array of events including comments, labels, assignments, cross-references, commits, and state changes that have occurred throughout the issue's lifecycle. You must provide the repository owner, repository name, and issue number as path parameters, and the response includes detailed information about each event such as the event type, actor, timestamp, and relevant metadata. This endpoint is particularly useful for tracking the complete history and activity of an issue, building audit trails, or creating visualizations of issue progression over time.

query.description: The number of results per page (max 100). For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

The page number of the results to fetch. For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

item.name: Get
GitHub Get an Issue Comment

request.name: GitHub Get an Issue Comment

description.content:
You can use the REST API to get comments on issues and pull requests. Every pull request is an issue, but not every issue is a pull request.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

item.name: GitHub Get an Issue Event

request.name: GitHub Get an Issue Event

description.content: The GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/events/{event_id} endpoint retrieves detailed information about a specific event associated with an issue in a GitHub repository. This operation requires the repository owner's username, the repository name, and the unique event identifier to fetch data about actions performed on issues such as labels being added or removed, assignments, milestone changes, comments, and state transitions. The endpoint returns a comprehensive event object containing metadata like the event type, actor who triggered the event, creation timestamp, and relevant context specific to that event type, making it useful for tracking the complete history and audit trail of changes made to issues within a repository.

item.name: GitHub Get an Issue

request.name: GitHub Get an Issue

description.content:
The API returns a [`301 Moved Permanently` status](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/guides/best-practices-for-using-the-rest-api#follow-redirects) if the issue was
[transferred](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/articles/transferring-an-issue-to-another-repository/) to another repository. If
the issue was transferred to or deleted from a repository where the authenticated user lacks read access, the API
returns a `404 Not Found` status. If the issue was deleted from a repository where the authenticated user has read
access, the API returns a `410 Gone` status. To receive webhook events for transferred and deleted issues, subscribe
to the [`issues`](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/webhooks/event-payloads/#issues) webhook.

**Note**: GitHub's REST API considers every pull request an issue, but not every issue is a pull request. For this
reason, "Issues" endpoints may return both issues and pull requests in the response. You can identify pull requests by
the `pull_request` key. Be aware that the `id` of a pull request returned from "Issues" endpoints will be an _issue id_. To find out the pull
request id, use the "[List pull requests](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/pulls/pulls#list-pull-requests)" endpoint.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

item.name: Issues
GitHub List Issue Comments for Repository

request.name: GitHub List Issue Comments for Repository

description.content:
You can use the REST API to list comments on issues and pull requests for a repository. Every pull request is an issue, but not every issue is a pull request.

By default, issue comments are ordered by ascending ID.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

query.description: The property to sort the results by.
Either `asc` or `desc`. Ignored without the `sort` parameter.
Only show results that were last updated after the given time. This is a timestamp in [ISO 8601](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601) format: `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ`.

The number of results per page (max 100). For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

The page number of the results to fetch. For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

item.name: GitHub List Issue Events for Repository

request.name: GitHub List Issue Events for Repository

description.content: The List Issue Events For Repository endpoint retrieves a paginated list of all issue events that have occurred within a specific repository. This GET operation accepts the repository owner and repository name as path parameters and returns event data such as when issues were opened, closed, reopened, labeled, assigned, or had other state changes applied to them. The endpoint supports pagination through query parameters and requires appropriate authentication to access private repositories, making it useful for tracking issue activity history, generating analytics, or monitoring repository engagement patterns across all issues rather than individual ones.

query.description: The number of results per page (max 100). For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

The page number of the results to fetch. For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

item.name: GitHub Get an Issue Event

request.name: GitHub Get an Issue Event

description.content: The GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/events/{event_id} endpoint retrieves detailed information about a specific event associated with an issue in a GitHub repository. This operation requires the repository owner's username, the repository name, and the unique event identifier to fetch data about actions performed on issues such as labels being added or removed, assignments, milestone changes, comments, and state transitions. The endpoint returns a comprehensive event object containing metadata like the event type, actor who triggered the event, creation timestamp, and relevant context specific to that event type, making it useful for tracking the complete history and audit trail of changes made to issues within a repository.

item.name: GitHub Get an Issue

request.name: GitHub Get an Issue

description.content:
The API returns a [`301 Moved Permanently` status](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/guides/best-practices-for-using-the-rest-api#follow-redirects) if the issue was
[transferred](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/articles/transferring-an-issue-to-another-repository/) to another repository. If
the issue was transferred to or deleted from a repository where the authenticated user lacks read access, the API
returns a `404 Not Found` status. If the issue was deleted from a repository where the authenticated user has read
access, the API returns a `410 Gone` status. To receive webhook events for transferred and deleted issues, subscribe
to the [`issues`](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/webhooks/event-payloads/#issues) webhook.

**Note**: GitHub's REST API considers every pull request an issue, but not every issue is a pull request. For this
reason, "Issues" endpoints may return both issues and pull requests in the response. You can identify pull requests by
the `pull_request` key. Be aware that the `id` of a pull request returned from "Issues" endpoints will be an _issue id_. To find out the pull
request id, use the "[List pull requests](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/pulls/pulls#list-pull-requests)" endpoint.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

variable.description: (Required) The account owner of the repository. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The name of the repository without the `.git` extension. The name is not case sensitive.
(Required) The number that identifies the issue.

Self-hosted Enterprise Server protocol

item.name: GitHub Update an Issue

request.name: GitHub Update an Issue

description.content:
Issue owners and users with push access can edit an issue.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

item.name: GitHub Add Assignees to an Issue

request.name: GitHub Add Assignees to an Issue

description.content: The Add Assignees To An Issue operation is a POST request to the /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/assignees endpoint that allows you to add one or more assignees to an existing GitHub issue. You must specify the repository owner, repository name, and issue number in the URL path, and provide an array of usernames in the request body to designate which GitHub users should be assigned to the issue. This operation requires appropriate permissions to modify the issue, and the specified users must have access to the repository. Upon successful execution, the API returns the updated issue object including the newly added assignees in the assignees field, making it useful for task delegation and collaboration workflows within GitHub repositories.

item.name: GitHub Remove Assignees from an Issue

request.name: GitHub Remove Assignees from an Issue

description.content: This API operation removes one or more assignees from a specified GitHub issue within a repository. By making a DELETE request to the endpoint with the repository owner, repository name, and issue number, you can provide a list of usernames in the request body to unassign those users from the issue. The authenticated user must have push access to the repository to perform this action, and only users who are currently assigned to the issue can be removed. Upon successful execution, the API returns the updated issue object with the modified assignees list, allowing you to confirm which users remain assigned to the issue.

description.content:
Checks if a user has permission to be assigned to a specific issue.

If the `assignee` can be assigned to this issue, a `204` status code with no content is returned.

Otherwise a `404` status code is returned.

item.name: GitHub List Issue Comments

request.name: GitHub List Issue Comments

description.content:
You can use the REST API to list comments on issues and pull requests. Every pull request is an issue, but not every issue is a pull request.

Issue comments are ordered by ascending ID.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

query.description: Only show results that were last updated after the given time. This is a timestamp in [ISO 8601](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601) format: `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ`.

The page number of the results to fetch. For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

item.name: GitHub List Issue Events

request.name: GitHub List Issue Events

description.content: The List Issue Events operation retrieves a chronological list of all events that have occurred on a specific issue within a GitHub repository. By making a GET request to /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/events, developers can access a comprehensive timeline of activities such as labels being added or removed, issue assignments, milestone changes, references from other issues or pull requests, and state changes like when the issue was closed or reopened. This endpoint is particularly useful for tracking the complete history and lifecycle of an issue, understanding how it evolved over time, and gathering data for analytics or auditing purposes. The response includes detailed information about each event type, the actor who triggered it, and the timestamp when it occurred, making it valuable for building issue management tools, generating reports, or implementing custom notification systems.

item.name: GitHub List Labels for an Issue

request.name: GitHub List Labels for an Issue

description.content: This GET endpoint retrieves all labels currently assigned to a specific issue in a GitHub repository. By providing the repository owner, repository name, and issue number in the URL path, the API returns an array of label objects associated with that issue. Each label object contains properties such as the label's name, color, description, and other metadata. This operation is useful for applications that need to display, filter, or process issues based on their categorization, as labels are commonly used in GitHub workflows to organize and classify issues by type, priority, status, or other custom criteria.

item.name: GitHub Add Labels to an Issue

request.name: GitHub Add Labels to an Issue

description.content: This POST endpoint adds one or more labels to a specific issue in a GitHub repository by specifying the repository owner, repository name, and issue number in the URL path. The request body should contain an array of label names to be added to the issue. Labels help categorize and organize issues for better project management and filtering. The authenticated user must have push access to the repository to add labels. Upon successful execution, the API returns a 200 status code with an array of all labels currently assigned to the issue, including both the newly added labels and any existing ones. This operation is idempotent, meaning adding a label that already exists on the issue will not create a duplicate.

item.name: GitHub Set Labels for an Issue

request.name: GitHub Set Labels for an Issue

description.content: The PUT operation on the /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/labels endpoint replaces all existing labels on a specific GitHub issue with a new set of labels provided in the request body. This is part of the GitHub Issues API and requires authentication with appropriate permissions to modify issues in the specified repository. Unlike PATCH operations that might add labels, this PUT method performs a complete replacement, removing any labels not included in the request. The operation takes the repository owner, repository name, and issue number as path parameters, and accepts an array of label names or objects in the request body to set as the complete label set for that issue.

request.name: GitHub Remove All Labels from an Issue

description.content: This API operation removes all labels that have been applied to a specific issue in a GitHub repository. By sending a DELETE request to the endpoint with the repository owner, repository name, and issue number, all existing labels associated with that issue will be cleared in a single operation. This is useful when you need to reset an issue's categorization or when reorganizing your labeling system, allowing you to start fresh without having to remove labels individually. The operation requires appropriate authentication and write access to the repository's issues.

item.name: GitHub Unlock an Issue

request.name: GitHub Unlock an Issue

description.content: The unlock issue operation is a DELETE request to the GitHub REST API endpoint /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/lock that removes the lock from a previously locked issue, allowing users to resume commenting and participating in the discussion. This operation requires authentication and appropriate permissions (push access to the repository) to execute successfully. Once unlocked, the issue becomes open for public interaction again according to the repository's normal access rules, and users who were previously prevented from commenting due to the lock can once again engage with the issue thread. The operation returns a 204 No Content status code upon successful completion and does not return any response body.

item.name: GitHub List Reactions for an Issue

request.name: GitHub List Reactions for an Issue

description.content: The List Reactions For An Issue endpoint allows developers to retrieve all reactions that have been added to a specific issue in a GitHub repository. By making a GET request to /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/reactions with the repository owner, repository name, and issue number as path parameters, users can fetch a paginated list of reaction objects containing details such as the reaction type (like thumbs up, thumbs down, laugh, confused, heart, hooray, rocket, or eyes), the user who created the reaction, and timestamps. This endpoint is useful for analyzing community sentiment, gathering feedback metrics, or displaying social engagement on issues within applications that integrate with GitHub's issue tracking system.

query.description: Returns a single [reaction type](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/reactions/reactions#about-reactions). Omit this parameter to list all reactions to an issue.

The number of results per page (max 100). For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

item.name: GitHub Delete an Issue Reaction

request.name: GitHub Delete an Issue Reaction

description.content: The DELETE operation on the /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/reactions/{reaction_id} endpoint allows authenticated users to remove a specific reaction from a GitHub issue. This endpoint requires the repository owner name, repository name, issue number, and the unique reaction ID to identify and delete the particular reaction. Users can only delete reactions they have created, and the operation requires appropriate authentication and permissions to access the repository and modify reactions. Upon successful deletion, the API returns a 204 No Content status code, confirming the reaction has been removed from the specified issue.

item.name: GitHub List Timeline Events for an Issue

request.name: GitHub List Timeline Events for an Issue

description.content: The GitHub Issues API endpoint `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/timeline` allows you to retrieve a chronological list of all timeline events associated with a specific issue in a repository. This GET operation returns a comprehensive array of events including comments, labels, assignments, cross-references, commits, and state changes that have occurred throughout the issue's lifecycle. You must provide the repository owner, repository name, and issue number as path parameters, and the response includes detailed information about each event such as the event type, actor, timestamp, and relevant metadata. This endpoint is particularly useful for tracking the complete history and activity of an issue, building audit trails, or creating visualizations of issue progression over time.

item.name: Labels
GitHub List Labels for an Issue

request.name: GitHub List Labels for an Issue

description.content: This GET endpoint retrieves all labels currently assigned to a specific issue in a GitHub repository. By providing the repository owner, repository name, and issue number in the URL path, the API returns an array of label objects associated with that issue. Each label object contains properties such as the label's name, color, description, and other metadata. This operation is useful for applications that need to display, filter, or process issues based on their categorization, as labels are commonly used in GitHub workflows to organize and classify issues by type, priority, status, or other custom criteria.

item.name: GitHub Add Labels to an Issue

request.name: GitHub Add Labels to an Issue

description.content: This POST endpoint adds one or more labels to a specific issue in a GitHub repository by specifying the repository owner, repository name, and issue number in the URL path. The request body should contain an array of label names to be added to the issue. Labels help categorize and organize issues for better project management and filtering. The authenticated user must have push access to the repository to add labels. Upon successful execution, the API returns a 200 status code with an array of all labels currently assigned to the issue, including both the newly added labels and any existing ones. This operation is idempotent, meaning adding a label that already exists on the issue will not create a duplicate.

item.name: GitHub Set Labels for an Issue

request.name: GitHub Set Labels for an Issue

description.content: The PUT operation on the /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/labels endpoint replaces all existing labels on a specific GitHub issue with a new set of labels provided in the request body. This is part of the GitHub Issues API and requires authentication with appropriate permissions to modify issues in the specified repository. Unlike PATCH operations that might add labels, this PUT method performs a complete replacement, removing any labels not included in the request. The operation takes the repository owner, repository name, and issue number as path parameters, and accepts an array of label names or objects in the request body to set as the complete label set for that issue.

item.name: GitHub Remove All Labels from an Issue

request.name: GitHub Remove All Labels from an Issue

description.content: This API operation removes all labels that have been applied to a specific issue in a GitHub repository. By sending a DELETE request to the endpoint with the repository owner, repository name, and issue number, all existing labels associated with that issue will be cleared in a single operation. This is useful when you need to reset an issue's categorization or when reorganizing your labeling system, allowing you to start fresh without having to remove labels individually. The operation requires appropriate authentication and write access to the repository's issues.

item.name: Lists
GitHub List Issue Comments for Repository

request.name: GitHub List Issue Comments for Repository

description.content:
You can use the REST API to list comments on issues and pull requests for a repository. Every pull request is an issue, but not every issue is a pull request.

By default, issue comments are ordered by ascending ID.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

query.description: The property to sort the results by.
Either `asc` or `desc`. Ignored without the `sort` parameter.
Only show results that were last updated after the given time. This is a timestamp in [ISO 8601](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601) format: `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ`.

The number of results per page (max 100). For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

item.name: GitHub List Issue Events for Repository

request.name: GitHub List Issue Events for Repository

description.content: The List Issue Events For Repository endpoint retrieves a paginated list of all issue events that have occurred within a specific repository. This GET operation accepts the repository owner and repository name as path parameters and returns event data such as when issues were opened, closed, reopened, labeled, assigned, or had other state changes applied to them. The endpoint supports pagination through query parameters and requires appropriate authentication to access private repositories, making it useful for tracking issue activity history, generating analytics, or monitoring repository engagement patterns across all issues rather than individual ones.

item.name: GitHub List Issue Comments

request.name: GitHub List Issue Comments

description.content:
You can use the REST API to list comments on issues and pull requests. Every pull request is an issue, but not every issue is a pull request.

Issue comments are ordered by ascending ID.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

item.name: GitHub List Issue Events

request.name: GitHub List Issue Events

description.content: The List Issue Events operation retrieves a chronological list of all events that have occurred on a specific issue within a GitHub repository. By making a GET request to /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/events, developers can access a comprehensive timeline of activities such as labels being added or removed, issue assignments, milestone changes, references from other issues or pull requests, and state changes like when the issue was closed or reopened. This endpoint is particularly useful for tracking the complete history and lifecycle of an issue, understanding how it evolved over time, and gathering data for analytics or auditing purposes. The response includes detailed information about each event type, the actor who triggered it, and the timestamp when it occurred, making it valuable for building issue management tools, generating reports, or implementing custom notification systems.

item.name: GitHub List Labels for an Issue

request.name: GitHub List Labels for an Issue

description.content: This GET endpoint retrieves all labels currently assigned to a specific issue in a GitHub repository. By providing the repository owner, repository name, and issue number in the URL path, the API returns an array of label objects associated with that issue. Each label object contains properties such as the label's name, color, description, and other metadata. This operation is useful for applications that need to display, filter, or process issues based on their categorization, as labels are commonly used in GitHub workflows to organize and classify issues by type, priority, status, or other custom criteria.

item.name: GitHub List Reactions for an Issue

request.name: GitHub List Reactions for an Issue

description.content: The List Reactions For An Issue endpoint allows developers to retrieve all reactions that have been added to a specific issue in a GitHub repository. By making a GET request to /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/reactions with the repository owner, repository name, and issue number as path parameters, users can fetch a paginated list of reaction objects containing details such as the reaction type (like thumbs up, thumbs down, laugh, confused, heart, hooray, rocket, or eyes), the user who created the reaction, and timestamps. This endpoint is useful for analyzing community sentiment, gathering feedback metrics, or displaying social engagement on issues within applications that integrate with GitHub's issue tracking system.

query.description: Returns a single [reaction type](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/reactions/reactions#about-reactions). Omit this parameter to list all reactions to an issue.

item.name: GitHub List Timeline Events for an Issue

request.name: GitHub List Timeline Events for an Issue

description.content: The GitHub Issues API endpoint `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/timeline` allows you to retrieve a chronological list of all timeline events associated with a specific issue in a repository. This GET operation returns a comprehensive array of events including comments, labels, assignments, cross-references, commits, and state changes that have occurred throughout the issue's lifecycle. You must provide the repository owner, repository name, and issue number as path parameters, and the response includes detailed information about each event such as the event type, actor, timestamp, and relevant metadata. This endpoint is particularly useful for tracking the complete history and activity of an issue, building audit trails, or creating visualizations of issue progression over time.

item.name: GitHub List Reactions for an Issue

request.name: GitHub List Reactions for an Issue

description.content: The List Reactions For An Issue endpoint allows developers to retrieve all reactions that have been added to a specific issue in a GitHub repository. By making a GET request to /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/reactions with the repository owner, repository name, and issue number as path parameters, users can fetch a paginated list of reaction objects containing details such as the reaction type (like thumbs up, thumbs down, laugh, confused, heart, hooray, rocket, or eyes), the user who created the reaction, and timestamps. This endpoint is useful for analyzing community sentiment, gathering feedback metrics, or displaying social engagement on issues within applications that integrate with GitHub's issue tracking system.

query.description: Returns a single [reaction type](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/reactions/reactions#about-reactions). Omit this parameter to list all reactions to an issue.

The number of results per page (max 100). For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

item.name: GitHub Delete an Issue Reaction

request.name: GitHub Delete an Issue Reaction

description.content: The DELETE operation on the /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/reactions/{reaction_id} endpoint allows authenticated users to remove a specific reaction from a GitHub issue. This endpoint requires the repository owner name, repository name, issue number, and the unique reaction ID to identify and delete the particular reaction. Users can only delete reactions they have created, and the operation requires appropriate authentication and permissions to access the repository and modify reactions. Upon successful deletion, the API returns a 204 No Content status code, confirming the reaction has been removed from the specified issue.

item.name: Remove
GitHub Remove Assignees from an Issue

request.name: GitHub Remove Assignees from an Issue

description.content: This API operation removes one or more assignees from a specified GitHub issue within a repository. By making a DELETE request to the endpoint with the repository owner, repository name, and issue number, you can provide a list of usernames in the request body to unassign those users from the issue. The authenticated user must have push access to the repository to perform this action, and only users who are currently assigned to the issue can be removed. Upon successful execution, the API returns the updated issue object with the modified assignees list, allowing you to confirm which users remain assigned to the issue.

item.name: GitHub Remove All Labels from an Issue

request.name: GitHub Remove All Labels from an Issue

description.content: This API operation removes all labels that have been applied to a specific issue in a GitHub repository. By sending a DELETE request to the endpoint with the repository owner, repository name, and issue number, all existing labels associated with that issue will be cleared in a single operation. This is useful when you need to reset an issue's categorization or when reorganizing your labeling system, allowing you to start fresh without having to remove labels individually. The operation requires appropriate authentication and write access to the repository's issues.

item.name: Repositories
GitHub List Issue Comments for Repository

request.name: GitHub List Issue Comments for Repository

description.content:
You can use the REST API to list comments on issues and pull requests for a repository. Every pull request is an issue, but not every issue is a pull request.

By default, issue comments are ordered by ascending ID.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

query.description: The property to sort the results by.
Either `asc` or `desc`. Ignored without the `sort` parameter.
Only show results that were last updated after the given time. This is a timestamp in [ISO 8601](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601) format: `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ`.

The number of results per page (max 100). For more information, see "[Using pagination in the REST API](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/using-pagination-in-the-rest-api)."

description.content: The List Issue Events For Repository endpoint retrieves a paginated list of all issue events that have occurred within a specific repository. This GET operation accepts the repository owner and repository name as path parameters and returns event data such as when issues were opened, closed, reopened, labeled, assigned, or had other state changes applied to them. The endpoint supports pagination through query parameters and requires appropriate authentication to access private repositories, making it useful for tracking issue activity history, generating analytics, or monitoring repository engagement patterns across all issues rather than individual ones.

item.name: Sets
GitHub Set Labels for an Issue

request.name: GitHub Set Labels for an Issue

description.content: The PUT operation on the /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/labels endpoint replaces all existing labels on a specific GitHub issue with a new set of labels provided in the request body. This is part of the GitHub Issues API and requires authentication with appropriate permissions to modify issues in the specified repository. Unlike PATCH operations that might add labels, this PUT method performs a complete replacement, removing any labels not included in the request. The operation takes the repository owner, repository name, and issue number as path parameters, and accepts an array of label names or objects in the request body to set as the complete label set for that issue.

item.name: Timelines
GitHub List Timeline Events for an Issue

request.name: GitHub List Timeline Events for an Issue

description.content: The GitHub Issues API endpoint `/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/timeline` allows you to retrieve a chronological list of all timeline events associated with a specific issue in a repository. This GET operation returns a comprehensive array of events including comments, labels, assignments, cross-references, commits, and state changes that have occurred throughout the issue's lifecycle. You must provide the repository owner, repository name, and issue number as path parameters, and the response includes detailed information about each event such as the event type, actor, timestamp, and relevant metadata. This endpoint is particularly useful for tracking the complete history and activity of an issue, building audit trails, or creating visualizations of issue progression over time.

item.name: Unlock
GitHub Unlock an Issue

request.name: GitHub Unlock an Issue

description.content: The unlock issue operation is a DELETE request to the GitHub REST API endpoint /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/lock that removes the lock from a previously locked issue, allowing users to resume commenting and participating in the discussion. This operation requires authentication and appropriate permissions (push access to the repository) to execute successfully. Once unlocked, the issue becomes open for public interaction again according to the repository's normal access rules, and users who were previously prevented from commenting due to the lock can once again engage with the issue thread. The operation returns a 204 No Content status code upon successful completion and does not return any response body.

item.name: Update
GitHub Update an Issue Comment

request.name: GitHub Update an Issue Comment

description.content:
You can use the REST API to update comments on issues and pull requests. Every pull request is an issue, but not every issue is a pull request.

This endpoint supports the following custom media types. For more information, see "[Media types](https://docs.github.com/enterprise-server@3.9/rest/using-the-rest-api/getting-started-with-the-rest-api#media-types)."

- **`application/vnd.github.raw+json`**: Returns the raw markdown body. Response will include `body`. This is the default if you do not pass any specific media type.
- **`application/vnd.github.text+json`**: Returns a text only representation of the markdown body. Response will include `body_text`.
- **`application/vnd.github.html+json`**: Returns HTML rendered from the body's markdown. Response will include `body_html`.
- **`application/vnd.github.full+json`**: Returns raw, text, and HTML representations. Response will include `body`, `body_text`, and `body_html`.

request.name: GitHub Check if User Can Be Assigned to Issue

description.content:
Checks if a user has permission to be assigned to a specific issue.

If the `assignee` can be assigned to this issue, a `204` status code with no content is returned.

Otherwise a `404` status code is returned.
